purpose: Defines the optional, non-blocking sibling-communication protocol for same-wave workers in a !p_track swarm. Workers may read other in-wave workers' progress files (specifically the shared_context object — exports, interfaces, patterns, notes) to align implementations, but they MUST NOT depend on sibling data and MUST NOT write to other workers' files. Specifies the SIBLING TASKS dispatch-prompt section that lists candidate sibling task IDs, the shared_context sub-schema, the mandatory log-entry-on-read and sibling_reads[] tracking requirements, and the strict rule that cross-wave coordination uses the formal upstream-dependency mechanism instead.
gotchas: Sibling reads are SUPPLEMENTARY only — tasks must be completable without any sibling data. Blocking or retrying for missing sibling progress files is a protocol violation.

Each worker owns EXACTLY ONE progress file — its own task_id.json. Writing to another worker's progress file is absolutely forbidden (would cause write conflicts since both workers overwrite full file).

Sibling reads are only valid for SAME-WAVE tasks. Cross-wave coordination must use the formal upstream dependency mechanism (upstream_deps.md), where the master injects upstream results into the dispatch prompt.
If a sibling's progress file doesn't exist yet, is empty, or lacks shared_context, proceed without it — the sibling may not have started yet.
Reading a sibling REQUIRES both an info log entry documenting what was found AND adding the task ID to sibling_reads[] — the dashboard renders dashed sibling-connection lines from sibling_reads[].
patterns: shared_context shape: { exports: string[], interfaces: string[], patterns: string[], notes: string } — populated as early as possible in the implementing stage so siblings can read it.
Read-only sibling access via {tracker_root}/dashboards/{dashboardId}/progress/{sibling_task_id}.json — same path schema as own progress file.
Sibling read tracking: every sibling read appended to sibling_reads[] (string array of task IDs) AND logged at info level even if the read returned no useful data.
Use case shape: sibling A defines an interface in shared_context.interfaces; sibling B reads it to type its own implementation, avoiding redundant interface definition.
conventions: sibling_reads[] elements are task ID strings (e.g., '2.1'), not objects.
shared_context.notes is free-form prose for context that doesn't fit exports/interfaces/patterns.
Single-writer invariant: only the worker whose task_id matches the file may write it.
